data.title: Buy 9 Barron's Better Bets (Than T-Bills) From 15 'Safer' Of 23 October Dividend Dogs
data.description:
Foreword

While half of this collection of Barron's Better Bets (BBB) is too pricey, or reveals somewhat skinny dividends, nine of the fifteen highest yield Dogs with the "Safest" dividends of the BBB are ready to buy. October finds Conagra (CAG), Verizon (VZ), Altria Group (MO), Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY), Truist Financial (TFC), UDR Inc (UDR), KeyCorp (KEY), Kimco Realty (KIM), and Regions Financial (RF), as the buyable top nine, living-up to the dogcatcher ideal. That is, each showed an annual dividend from $1K invested exceeding its single share price.

The remaining six of the October "Safer" fifteen have prices exceeding the dividend returned from $1k invested. Of the six, ONEOK (OKE) is closest to the mark with a price within $4.77 of equaling the dividend paid from $1k invested.

With renewed downside market pressure of 48.11%, it will be possible for all ten of the top BBB "Safer" dividend dogs to become elite fair-priced with their annual yield (from $1K invested) meeting or exceeding their single share prices.

[See a summary of top ten fair-priced October BBB Dogs (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and the aforementioned nine as "safer" choices in the Afterword, at the bottom of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 20.73% To 38.12% Net Gains From Top-Ten BBB Dogs By October 2026

Five of ten top BBB Dogs (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top-ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this October, 2025 yield-based forecast for BBB dogs, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 50% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the 2025-26 data points for the projections below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to October 2026 were:

ONEOK (OKE) was projected to net $381.15 based on the median of target price estimates from 20 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 1% over the market as a whole.

LyondellBasell (LYB) was projected to net $372.00, based on the median of target price estimates from 20 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 12% less than the market as a whole.

Verizon was projected to net $271.89, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 24 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 64% under the market as a whole.

KeyCorp was projected to net $268.22, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 23 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 15% over the market as a whole.

Philip Morris International (PM) was projected to net $256.97, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 18 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 54% less than the market as a whole.

Bristol-Myers Squibb was projected to net $237.87, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 26 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 67% less than the market as a whole.

Pfizer (PFE) was projected to net $218.17, based on the median of target price estimates from 24 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 45% less than the market as a whole.

UDR Inc was projected to net $212.19, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 23 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 12% under the market as a whole.

Regions Financial was projected to net $207.57, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 24 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 3% more than the market as a whole.

Equity Residential was projected to net $207.29 based on the median of target prices estimated by 26 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 10% less than the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 26.33% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten BBB stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 24% less than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

October BBB Total Returns

October BBB Broker Targets

The October, 2025 BBB 23 By Yield

Actionable Conclusion: October Top Ten BBB Stocks By Yield Ranged 4.81% To 12.12% Per YCharts

Top ten October BBB Dogs occupied five of eleven Morningstar sectors.

In first place, was the lone basic materials dog, LyondellBasell [1]. Then, two consumer staple stalwarts, placed second, and fifth, Conagra Brands [2], and Altria Group [5].

Thereafter, two health care sector members placed third and seventh, Pfizer [3], and Bristol-Myers Squibb [7].

A lone communication services sector placed fourth, Verizon [4]. Then, one energy stock placed sixth, ONEOK [6].

Two real estate sector dogs took the eighth, and ninth positions: Realty Income (O) [8], and Simon Property Group (SPG) [9].

Finally, another loner from the financial sector placed tenth, Truist, [10], and completed the October Dogs of the BBB by yield.

One of 23 October, 2025 BBB Placed in the Top Ten by Yield, Target, and Return

  • ONEOK Inc in the energy sector was the only October BBB member to place in the top ten in all three scoring categories.

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The top graph above shows the relative strengths of the top ten October BBB dogs by dividend and price as of market close 10/21/2025.

BBB Prices per Dividend Dollar

The full chart above reveals twenty-three BBB dogs subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock. The least expensive dog of the BBB is LyondellBasell at $8.25 per dividend dollar and the most expensive is Entergy at $39.86 per dividend dollar.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Six of Twenty-Three BBB Dogs Show Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends. Six BBB so infected are: LyondellBasell, Federal Realty, Kinder Morgan, Phillip Morris, Williams Companies, and Entergy. These are considered unsafe to buy. Furthermore their infection explains how the are missing from the IDEAL "safer" dividend dog reckoning.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, tariffs, trade-offs, war-games, rants, and uncertainty.

Price Drops, Dividend Increases or Dividend Splits Could Get All Ten BBB Dogs Back to "Fair Price" Rates For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, seven in the top ten are already ideally priced. Beside those, three more top-yield stocks are in striking distance of getting there.

The alternative, of course, could be that these companies raise or split their dividends. That may be too much to ask in these highly disrupted, inflationary, recessionary, yet cash-rich, and volatile times. Mr. Market is far more effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts, just watch and buy when your targeted stock price moves to a sweet spot.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 16.99% To 33.14% Top Ten BBB Upsides To October, 2025; (32) One -3.10% Downsider Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 4.40% Advantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 BBB Dogs As of October 21, 2026

Ten-top BBB dogs were culled by yield for this monthly-update. Yield (dividend / price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted-above, top-ten BBB dogs selected 10/21/25 by yield, represented six of eleven sectors. (Consumer Cyclical, Consumer Discretionary, Industrials, Technology, and Utilities, missed the top-ten cut.)

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of the Ten Highest-Yield BBB Dogs (34) To Deliver 21.72% Vs. (35) 20.80% Net Gains by All Ten Come October 21, 2026

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten BBB Dividend kennel by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 4.40% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The ninth lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, ONEOK, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 38.12%.

The five lowest-priced BBB top-yield dogs for October were: Conagra; Pfizer; Verizon; Truist Financial; Bristol-Myers Squibb, with prices ranging from $18.62 to $44.54.

Five higher-priced Dow top-yield dogs as of October 21 were: LyondellBasell; Realty Income; Altria; ONEOK; Simon Property, with prices ranging from $45.21 to $178.72.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As summer cools and the market shivers, the feckless Fed-fueled slide and slithering presidential trade pronouncements add momentum toward 2026 (with the exception of a few overly speculative and meme stocks) look for all of the top ten to twenty-three BBB Dogs to become Fair-priced… slowly, but surely, or real quick!

Afterword

Buy 9 of 15 'Safer' Dividend October Dogs of the BBB

Late-October 2025, 15 of the 23 BBB were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns with free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields. Then nine IDEAL of the fifteen "safer" showed dividend yields from $1000 invested exceeding the price of a single share.

How BBB 'Safer' Dividend Vs. Price Results Could Produce A "Fair Price" Result For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that seven dividends from $1K invested in top ten 'safer' dividend BBB dogs conform to the dogcatcher standard of dividends from $1,000 invested being equal to (or greater than) the price of a single share. Those seven ideal dogs were Conagra, Verizon, Altria, Bristol-Myers Squibb, Truist, Regions Financial, UDR, and KeyCorp.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal the single share price of each stock; a so-called "fair price."

A market correction of only 48.11% could put all ten share prices of these ten "safer" survivors above or equal to their dividend payouts from $1k invested.

There are, of course, other ways for dividends from $1K invested to exceed the price of a single share of stock. That would be for the boards of directors these companies to raise dividends or make stock splits to the dogcatcher ideal level. That's a lot to ask with all the corporate funds doing so much "good" elsewhere.

BBB IDEAL "Safer" Dividends From Positive Return & Cash Flow Exceeding Dividend Yields

Adding the IDEAL dividends from stocks outside the top ten BBB list produces the above list of nine.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher BBB 23 Updates, out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" BBB dog dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational and educational purposes only and should not be construed to constitute investment advice. Nothing contained herein shall constitute a solicitation, recommendation or endorsement to buy or sell any security. Prices and returns on equities in this article except as noted are listed without consideration of fees, commissions, taxes, penalties, or interest payable due to purchasing, holding, or selling same.

Graphs and charts were compiled by Rydlun & Co., LLC from data derived from Indexarb; YCharts; finance.yahoo.com; analyst mean target price by YCharts. Open source dog art from dividenddogcatcher.com.

data.title: Meta Blinks On AI
data.description:
This is a brief follow-up to an article I wrote on September 16th titled "How To Play The Coming AI Capex Bust".

The article noted that most AI investment is coming from 4 hyperscalers. It also noted that, while AI itself is here to stay and will have a massive impact, the AI capex boom will likely end within a few years. The reasons for this are summarized below.

  1. Future AI will need less power, as indicated by DeepSeek and soon Spiking Neural Networks.
  2. Power constraints will delay many data center projects for years.
  3. Efficiency gains will reduce the need to invest in infrastructure.
  4. New technologies, such as quantum computing, will compete with data centers and lower costs.
  5. No proof of an acceptable ROI yet; returns for most are quite small so far.
  6. CFOs and investors will wait only so long for worthwhile payoffs.
  7. NIMBY pushback against data centers picking up.
  8. New technology often takes longer than you think to pay off, as proven by the internet in 2000 and autonomous driving (a form of AI) over the past 10 years.
  9. Numerous unsolved challenges in harnessing the power of the data centers to actually solve problems.
  10. Extreme AI-related stock valuations.
  11. Possible pushback from governments over workers losing jobs.

Of the above, I believe that numbers 5 and 6, which are related, are the biggest issues in the near term.

On October 21, 2025, Meta announced 600 job cuts. They noted that most of the staff affected will be shifted to other positions within Meta (NASDAQ:META). This appears to be in relation to Meta’s launch of its Llama 4 AI language model in April, which has been unsuccessful to date. Reallocating AI jobs is a sign that ROI is becoming more important. This is critical because industry-wide spending to date has not taken ROI into account. Once that starts, the returns need to be evident to continue spending growth.

A bigger deal may be an announcement by Meta the day before. Meta is building a massive new data center to be 80% owned by Blue Owl Capital. In the past, the hyperscalers like Meta financed most of their data centers internally. By doing this transaction, Meta is taking some risk off its balance sheet. This is a sign of hedging their bets.

Meta had been the most aggressive of the hyperscalers. It currently expects to spend $114 to $118 billion on AI initiatives in 2025 and has indicated it may spend as much as $600 billion over the next three years. Over the summer, Meta reportedly paid 9 figure sums to attract AI talent from its rivals. This appears to be a pullback on two fronts in a relatively short period of time.

Meanwhile, others are still racing forward. OpenAI has become the most aggressive in AI, or is trying to be. It recently announced deals of over $1 trillion for data center capacity. Unlike the hyperscalers (Meta, Alphabet, Amazon, and Microsoft), OpenAI has neither the balance sheet nor the cash flow to fund even a small fraction of these plans. The largest investor in this space, SoftBank, is way too small to put a dent in it. Investors are increasingly going to want to see a pathway to worthwhile returns. OpenAI appears to be angling for investment from the only viable source out there, the U.S. government. Well, I guess there’s also the Saudis.

Meta itself will be reporting its earnings on October 29th. In addition to billions of dollars being spent on data centers, it is shifting its AI toward something it calls Meta Superintelligence Labs (MSL). Many of the 600 jobs being eliminated will likely end up there. MSL is aiming for an AI capable of surpassing human intellect. Its goal is to advance AI capabilities in areas such as reasoning, problem-solving, and general intelligence.

Takeaway

It’s premature to call the AI capex wave over or even close to slowing. In my prior article, I said 2027 may be the peak. But some cracks are starting to show. Meta had been the most aggressive this year, outspending the other hyperscalers and paying huge sums for talent. Meta has backed off big ideas before. In fact, they changed their name to Meta from Facebook to pursue their last big idea. Meta's pause appears related to an AI-related failure, the launch of Llama 4. There will undoubtedly be more failures by others over the next year or two.

Keep watching Meta, the other hyperscalers, and the U.S. government to see what they announce. Also watch revenues for Amazon’s (AMZN) AWS, Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) Cloud, and Microsoft’s (MSFT) Azure. Azure is showing revenue acceleration, up 39% YoY last quarter from 29% in 2024. AWS’s revenue growth rate is still declining. Google Cloud growth was 32% last quarter, up from 29% one year earlier. So, the growth rate overall is increasing, but not by enough to justify the current level of investment, except perhaps by Microsoft. In the meantime, avoid stocks that are most tied to AI capex. I provided a list in my prior article.

data.title: Quantum Corporation: Quantum Only In The Name
data.description:
Introduction

Yesterday’s trading session brought another reminder of how detached markets can become from reality, especially when discussing “quantum” concepts. Investors seem to love the word. Quantum Corporation (NASDAQ:QMCO), a company that has nothing to do with actual quantum computing, jumped into double digits after rumors spread that the U.S. government might take equity stakes in “key quantum players.” IonQ, Inc. (IONQ), D-Wave Quantum Inc. (QBTS), Rigetti Computing, Inc. (RGTI), Arqit Quantum Inc. (ARQQ), Quantum Computing Inc. (QUBT), and Quantum Corporation have all suddenly surged. And I could understand some of them flying, but QMCO has nothing to do with it.

Later, the U.S. Commerce Department denied that it wants a stake in quantum firms for government equity participation, clarifying that while the Trump administration has taken stakes in strategic companies like Intel Corporation (INTC) and MP Materials Corp. (MP), it is not negotiating any such stakes with quantum computing companies. But still, most stocks closed high and added some more after hours.

I am really fascinated and concerned at the same time about how easily investors believed it and went on to buy. Today, I want to discuss whether a government stake in quantum stocks is at all possible and what role Quantum Corporation plays in this story.

The “Quantum Stake” News

It all started with a Wall Street Journal piece claiming that the Trump administration was in talks to take equity positions in several quantum computing firms, including IonQ, Rigetti, and D-Wave. The logic was that since the administration already took ownership stakes in Intel and MP Materials (companies considered vital to national security), they might do the same with emerging “strategic” technologies like quantum computing. Of course, the stocks surged on the news.

But not too long after, CNBC reported an official denial:

The Commerce Department is not currently negotiating equity stakes with quantum computing companies.

Firstly, even if the initial claim about the stake had been true, Quantum Corp. should not have been among the companies that would benefit. QMCO doesn’t make quantum computers. It doesn’t even use them. It’s a data storage and video management company, specializing in unstructured data storage, file systems, and high-performance video workflows. Its main clients include enterprises in media, entertainment, and surveillance. It does not even have the revenue from research, which all the other companies have, because they actually have something quantum-related.

Second point that I wanted to make is that quantum companies are so ahead of their valuation that it would go against any common sense for the government to take a stake there. It’s like taking a stake in hype around something. Why would the government find it useful? If the government wanted to do something with quantum, it would be much easier to partner with some research centers or universities and to fund their own research focused on the government’s goals. You don’t believe me on this? Take a look at the stake of institutions in IONQ and QBTS. It’s really small compared to the stakes in progressive tech companies. Usually, it’s at least 80%. Government is the most rational institution when it comes to investments and spending, so I wouldn’t hope for it. Not until these companies trade at sane valuation multiples.

What Quantum Corp. Actually Does & Financials

According to its latest 10-Q and earnings release, Quantum delivers end-to-end data management solutions for AI-era workloads, meaning systems that help enterprises manage huge video or analytics datasets efficiently. Its product mix includes storage appliances, file systems, and long-term archiving software. I can’t understand where qubit processing and quantum technology are in all of this. The company’s history dates back over four decades, long before “quantum computing” was even a thing, so I assume Quantum in the name is to attract attention to the stock.

For the quarter ended June 30, 2025, revenue came in at $64.3 million, down 11% year-over-year from $72.3 million. Gross profit was $22.7 million (35% margin), down from $27.1 million (37% margin). The company reported a GAAP net loss of $17.2 million, or –$1.87 per share, and an adjusted EBITDA of –$6.5 million. This company is deeply unprofitable. The new CEO, Hugues Meyrath, said he’s focused on “improving cost structure and balance sheet,” which basically means he tries to stop the business from bleeding and losing cash, but for now, it doesn’t seem to be working.

Cash flow from operations was negative $16.9 million last quarter, while the company’s cash balance is $37.5 million against $104 million of term debt. A major red flag, the company needs to look for money, and to do it fast.

Just a year ago, Quantum had around 4.8 million shares outstanding. Today it’s at 13.3 million. That’s almost a 3x dilution in less than a year.

Insiders, of course, are selling, and no one buys. CEO Hugues Meyrath, CFO Laura Nash, and multiple former executives have all dumped shares in recent months, at prices between $7 and $12.

Institutional ownership is barely 7.6%, insiders hold 1.7%, and the rest (over 90%) belongs to retail investors. That’s a textbook setup for volatility and retail-driven squeezes. In general, every metric of this company that I checked looks like a major red flag. You have a short interest of only 17%, which is very low for the company in such a bad shape. It means there is almost no chance for a hype-driven short squeeze if you hoped for it.

Volatile sector

I have been interested in the quantum sector for years, casually reading and learning about it as much as my non-physics brain can process. Not so long ago, I wrote an article, “A Look At The Quantum Computing Sector, With IonQ At The Center Of The Debate”, which discussed the sector in detail and explained it in simple language. If you are an investor already or want to become one, you must go and read this piece. After doing so, you will understand why I deny that Quantum Corporation has something to do with quantum technology. After this, I wrote another piece: “D-Wave Quantum: The Only Quantum You Can Use Today, But I Would Still Sell”. Both IONQ and QBTS are much closer to building something real, but I still rated them as a sell (I noted that I did not mean to short, only that I would close my position) because the valuations reflected investor interest instead of real developments. After brief rallies (IonQ up 20%, D-Wave up nearly 70%), both stocks gave up most of their gains. So while there was an upside since my article, the downside was as big, and while some investors were lucky, others are now in the deep red.

This is exactly the kind of volatility that defines speculative sectors where excitement runs ahead of progress. These companies are not bad, they really try to innovate and to capture the market, but he technology isn’t there yet. Quantum computing remains experimental. Even optimistic projections from Google and others admit that there’s no useful quantum computer today. All the tasks can still be performed faster and more efficiently by traditional high-end GPUs. But once again, now I am back to discussing other stocks and not Quantum Corporation. What they do, and try to do, should be made with other technologies. Using quantum for these tasks is as unnecessary as building a robot that will build for you a stove for cooking your dinner. You can just buy a stove from a shop and save a lot of time and money.

Conclusion

Quantum Corporation trades at a forward Price-to-Sales ratio of just 0.49 and an EV/Sales of 0.76, objectively cheap. But there’s a reason for that: the company keeps losing money, diluting shareholders, and burning cash. It’s not a quantum computing stock, not a turnaround story, and not an obvious value play. It’s simply a heavily indebted data storage company that got caught in a wave of mistaken identity.

You can build, store, and analyze data perfectly well with existing GPU-based systems. There’s no need for “quantum” anything here, just efficient infrastructure and smart execution. Until Quantum Corp. shows a path to sustainable profitability and stops leaning on equity dilution to survive, this remains a speculative trap.

As with every speculative stock, I rate it as a Sell, but I wouldn’t short it. It’s too speculative, and the hype can take this stock up. It won’t have anything to do with performance or execution.

data.title: Matson Is Quietly Winning The Pacific Trade Game
data.description:
Matson (NYSE:MATX) still stays that one stable and boring name in the shipping sector; the company does not compete primarily by scale, but earns from niche, high value routes. After a very strong Q1 when profit was double (from $36 to $72 million), the second one brought problems - Chinese traffic has decreased, rates are under pressure, and the seasonal peak is putting pressure. But Matson is not reacting with panic, but discipline: the profit fell quite less than I was expecting after tariffs, cash flows remain strong, and the company is still increasing dividends and doing share buybacks.

What I Expect In Q3

In Q2 2025, Matson's results showed a natural slowdown after a strong year-start. After Q1, which was very good, when the profit was double, today we see the situation is changing. The company's consolidated revenue was $830.5 million, compared to $847.4 million a year ago. Net income fell to $94.7 million, while a year ago it was $113.2 million (-16.3%).

I expect the company to stay naturally cool in Q3. The main thing is rates and volume on Chinese routes. The company itself warned that Ocean Transportation profitability will be significantly lower than a year ago, when it was $226.9 million. I believe it will even go down to the $150-180 million range, depending on how much the tariffs are and if the volumes stabilize. The logistics part, in my opinion, should remain stable and generate the same or slightly lower profits as last year.

I believe that the company will maintain shareholder value creation with dividends and continued share buybacks. The domestic ocean services (Hawaii, Alaska, Guam) should remain stable and generate several hundred million in annual EBITDA. But keep in mind, what I expect is pure speculation, and the company could turn in any direction.

The Market

The market remains kind of mixed - logistics sector is balancing between demand recovery and new geopolitical disruptions. In October 2025, US government decided to increase port fees for Chinese built and Chinese-operated ships, which caused a chain reaction in the Pacific Ocean market. The ships are now facing a fee (levies) for entering US ports, around $120 per container or $18 per net ton, and the fee can be applied up to five times per year. Indirectly, Matson is affected as well, as it is servicing one of the most important US-China routes through Long Beach.

In short term, these fees increased rates: SCFI jumped 12.9% week-over-week. However, I do not believe this is sustainable growth, as part of exporters were rushing to ship cargo before fees applied. In the long term this means that US importers will have to seek alternative supply chains, especially from Southeast Asia. Matson, which has experience in the Pacific Ocean's south zone, will keep on benefiting from this if they are going to maintain their diversification routes in Vietnam or potentially in Philippines.

In Q2 2025, they announced that transshipment share in Chinese routes increased around 21%, compared to 13% in Q1. This growth is mainly driven by stronger ties with Vietnam, I believe they will try to access the Philippines and Malaysia, which would allow clients from these countries to reach US West Coast ports through the Matson network. The Vietnam expedited service is allowing faster goods transportation from Vietnam to Long Beach through Matson terminals. This service is not only shortening transit time, but also helping clients avoid dependency on Chinese routes, which, as I said, in the last few months are experiencing pressure.

However, the short-term risk remains: increased port fees, Matson might experience higher operational costs in the Long Beach terminal in the following quarters and lower rates of competition in Asian markets. If the tariff war between the US and China gets deeper, the company would have to manage its revenue structure more carefully, as its Ocean Transportation segment is most dependent on these flows.

New Ships - A Catalyst

Matson is actively renewing their fleet, trying to reduce fuel costs and maintain competitiveness. In September 2024, they started new shipbuilding (the first steel cutting) for Aloha Class, which is expected to be one of the most modern US ocean container ships. I believe this is a good move, LNG ships reduce fuel costs, have better effectiveness, and lower emissions. Given that the environmental regulation in the shipping sector is always putting pressure, these ships should help solve some of the issues.

The contract for 3 ships is worth $1 billion, or around $333 million per ship, but this is investing in margin stability for the long term. LNG engines, could reduce fuel costs up to 25% if we look at how they work in the industry, and dual fuel systems should let them stay flexible in fluctuating fuel markets. Matson expects that these ships will start working on the Hawaii route in early 2027, replacing older diesel ships.

I believe this project is not only a modernization, but a reputational step too: Matson is positioning itself as an environmentally friendly US shipping leader that is investing in future technology, not a short-term profit. This gives some attractiveness for long-term investors, as the company shows stability and value.

SSA Terminals

One of the most important catalysts, and the one investors tend to forget, is that the company is involved in the SSAT (SSA Terminals) joint venture. Matson holds a 35% interest in SSAT, which is a strategic partnership with SSA Marine, operating cargo terminals on the US West Coast (including Long Beach, Oakland, and Seattle). SSAT gives Matson greater control over the value chain - from ship to port—giving it a competitive advantage against operators, that are dependent on third party terminals. Also, as I mentioned, port fees are increasing and logistics are getting disrupted, and the whole market is quite unstable. Being in such partnerships gives operational flexibility and lower waiting costs.

This partnership also contributes, as in Q2 2025, Matson's share of SSAT's contribution was $7.3 million, and that is up from around $1.2 million a year ago. I think that, in the market, where port fees are rising and logistics bottlenecks are increasing, having a stake in terminal operations gives Matson operational flexibility and helps, at least by a little bit, to hedge against disruptions and waiting costs. While, yes, this is not the largest segment, SSAT is a meaningful diversification and cash-flow benefit, which, in my opinion, supports Matson's broader strategy of shareholder value creation, such as dividends and share buybacks.

Valuation

If we look at the valuation, I believe we can see that Matson stays one of the most stable companies. P/E (FWD) 8.82, almost two times lower than Star Bulk (SBLK) at 13.81 and higher than ZIM, which is currently traded at 4.42. This shows that the market already prices it as a middle cycle, lower risk operator, which is not dependent on extreme tariff jumps.

EV/EBITDA (TTM) - 4.82, shows that Matson is quite effective, if we compare it to DAC (4.19) or CMRE (4.09), this is good, company is generating solid cash flow, which lets them maintain such a ratio. Price/Book is at 1.13, this one is interesting, because it is rare for a high-quality company.

Another very strong metric is PEG GAAP [TTM], which is just 0.11, meaning that the shares are quite cheap compared to growth potential. If we compare it to ZIM, which is currently experiencing a big profitability swing, Matson looks cheap. Before I am getting fired, at, yes, PEG can fluctuate sharply in cyclical industries, Matson's low ratio shows how the market still discounts its stable earnings base.

Even though ZIM and Star Bulk operate under quite different shipping models, these companies provide some context on how the market prices earnings and risk across the sector.

All in all, I believe the company looks cheap, its quality is good for the price, and it's not the cheapest share in the sector, but it's the only one that has a strong balance, dividends, and lower risk.

Summary

Matson remains one of the few US shipping companies that can generate profits even when the cycle is getting weaker. The company has a clear advantage - controlled niche routes (Hawaii, Alaska, Long Beach-Asia). Even though Chinese flows are currently weak, the growing transshipment part and Vietnam services are expanding the operational base. New Aloha Class LNG ships will let them decrease costs and increase margins from early 2027, and SAAT terminal business helps to stabilize cash flows.

I believe Matson is quite attractive: P/E (FWD) only 8.8x, EV/EBITDA around 4.8x, PEG 0.11. This is cheap quality. The market currently does not price in diversification into Southeast Asia and an environmentally friendly fleet. I am going to be maintaining my buy rating.

data.title: My Favorite ETF Portfolio Picks For A Low-Stress Retirement
data.description:
Introduction

I have to say that I am truly blessed with a fantastic community of readers. For example, my "It Looks Like A Bubble, It Feels Like A Bubble, But it Isn’t" article got more than 250 comments, as everyone had something to say.

That’s fantastic, for a number of reasons. The most obvious one is that it adds tremendous value to my articles, as I try to reply to as many people as possible. It also keeps me straight, as I know whatever I write will be harshly criticized by some incredibly skilled people.

Moreover, sometimes people give me fantastic ideas, especially when it comes to exploring investments for specific purposes. This includes a comment I got on October 16 that I was allowed to share with you:

Great read Leo … I read everything of yours but don’t comment very often. I always think to myself I wish I had been at your investment level when I was your age. As I have mentioned before I am working towards retirement in the next year and am transitioning to a 50% etf/50% individual stocks portfolio for income. Although I have accumulated a nice nest egg I still need a decent dividend from my portfolio. That is where I struggle with the etf side … could you give your thoughts on what you would do on the etf side if you were retiring and could invest in ETF’s and wanted some income from the etf side . As always, great work!! - Comment (emphasis added)

It’s a fantastic comment, as it allows me to cover a lot of ground.

For starters, the 50/50 approach is something I have brought up a few times in the past. This includes an article on April 18, where I called the 50/50 Dividend Strategy "One Of My Favorite Ways To Build Wealth" in the title.

Back then, I wrote that if I were to manage a strategy like this, I would keep it super simple, buying two ETFs that provide dividend growth and dividend income, and a bunch of stocks with limited overlap with these ETFs for both diversification and additional alpha. I brought up the Schwab Growth ETF (SCHG) and the Schwab Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). However, there are more that would fit my views.

To go back to the comment, it perfectly describes that our investment goals change as we get older and our life circumstances change. In this case, the reader has a "nice nest-egg" but requires additional income. I’m not sure what the total dollar amounts are, but I have enough to work with, as I can somewhat imagine what the situation looks like.

That’s also what makes writing certain articles so hard. As I always say, the older we get, the bigger the differences are in our finances. While we all have the same net worth in kindergarten (sorry for these silly examples), once we get older, the differences really become visible, as some are multi-millionaire business owners, while others work paycheck to paycheck or manage to save a few thousand for retirement.

Meanwhile, others inherit a lot of money, while others are "forced" to take care of their family.

Then there are differences we cannot always impact. For example, some make it to the age of 90 without major issues, while others are less fortunate and leave us way too early.

Essentially, it all boils down to one of three scenarios during retirement:

  • We can still increase our wealth during retirement. This is obviously the most desired situation, as we can enjoy income to supplement our Social Security and other cash flows, while still growing our portfolios through dividend growth stocks and other investments. Even Warren Buffett still invests with a long-term mindset.
  • Preserve principal. This is the situation where we perfectly balance expenses and portfolio income. Here, it’s critical to pick safe stocks with inflation protection, as any capital losses or long-term purchasing power declines could cause serious issues.
  • Spending the principal. This is the situation where investors are forced to spend their principal to generate additional cash flows. It’s quite common, which is why the 4% Rule exists. The funny thing is that I know a few very wealthy people who have a similar mindset. They are too rich to see their wealth decline (even small increases in the stock market have a huge impact on their portfolios), yet they try to enjoy their wealth as much as possible by buying really expensive cars and assets (you only live once).

Anyway, for the purpose of this article, I decided to find a perfect balance between growth and income. I present a 50/50 strategy for retirees with a focus on the ETF selection.

I will show you what I would buy and how we can make changes and adjustments, as this allows me to reach a wider audience.

So, as we have a lot to discuss, let’s get to it!

However, I need to mention two things. The first thing is that none of this is financial advice. This goes for every article. I provide food for thought. Retirement investment is very serious and requires support from someone who knows your personal situation. Even if I were legally allowed to, I could not provide tailored advice unless I knew your entire financial situation.

The second thing is that I am truly thankful for all the comments and the engagement. Without them, articles like this one would not be possible.

Thank you!

Building A Well-Balanced Retirement Portfolio

In general, building a portfolio is a bit like playing with Lego bricks. The opportunities are endless once we can get our hands on a nice variety of bricks. Just like Lego bricks, opportunities on the stock market are endless, as we can literally pick from thousands of single stocks and ETFs. On top of that, there’s a thick layer of derivatives and structured products that all come with a great sales pitch from whoever offers them.

In today’s article, we can narrow it all down as we look for income.

Personally, when I construct dividend retirement accounts, I mainly look for an average yield of roughly 5.5%, which I consider the sweet spot. In other words, I think we can buy income close to 5.5% without having to take elevated risks that can end up pushing us into so-called "sucker yields." In fact, I have focused my entire future retirement on a 5.0% (instead of 5.5%) yield. If I feel like I can achieve my financial goals with a 5.0% yield, I’m set.

In this article, I decided to go with four different ETFs:

  • Dividend growth. These stocks provide long-term sustainability, above-average inflation protection, and exposure to some of the biggest trends at the moment.
  • Additional income. I like the covered call strategy, especially at the market’s elevated valuation. As I explained in an interview with Seeking Alpha earlier this year, I believe a very conservative strategy that includes selling covered calls can make a lot of sense in the years ahead to improve the income profile of a portfolio. However, because there are a lot of irresponsible ways to do it, I am ultra-focused on safety here (even more than usual).
  • Midstream infrastructure. As I have written in prior articles (like this one), I believe pipeline companies are some of the best long-term investments an income-seeking investor can make. So, without a doubt, they get a special place in this article, as they combine income with growth and strong secular growth from the data center buildout, energy exports, and increasingly healthy cash flows.
  • Broader yield exposure. In order to further diversify the portfolio and add income, I’m adding special income. After all, we also need to keep correlations subdued. If we buy too many correlated assets, we’re defeating the purpose of careful asset selection in a concentrated portfolio.

Here’s what I came up with.

For dividend growth, I’m picking the Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (CGDV). Although I really dislike its 0.33% total expense ratio, it’s warranted, as this ETF has really made a name for itself in recent years, which also comes with a five-star rating from Morningstar.

Essentially, this ETF aims to produce consistent income above the yield of the S&P 500 by focusing on high-quality assets. However, it is by no means a higher-income ETF, as it yields just 1.4%. The S&P 500 yields a mere 1.1% due to its massive Big Tech exposure.

This ETF holds just 52 holdings with a turnover ratio of 29%, meaning less than a third of its stocks were replaced so far this year. Slightly more than 90% of its exposure is invested in the United States, with a focus on information technology, industrials, healthcare, and consumer discretionary stocks.

As we can see below, it owns some of the highest-quality companies in areas with secular growth, including Microsoft (MSFT), NVIDIA (NVDA), RTX Corp. (RTX), Broadcom (AVGO), Eli Lilly (LLY), and GE Aerospace (GE). None of these accounts for more than 6.2% of its total exposure, which prevents this ETF from turning into an AI ETF.

Since its inception in February of 2022, it has returned 17.8% per year, beating the 14.7% annual return of the S&P 500 by a decent margin. I am obviously not sure if this will continue, but given my view on the market and the holdings of this ETF, I think it has a clear edge.

Another dividend growth investment that could make sense is the Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF (VIG), which yields 1.6% with a total expense ratio of 0.05%.

For high-quality dividend income, I like the SCHD Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF. This ETF has gotten a lot of headwinds from dissatisfied investors in recent years due to its underperformance. It currently has just three stars from Morningstar.

This somewhat poor rating is the result of the fact that value stocks simply didn’t perform well in recent years. SCHD owns a lot of them. As we can see below, this includes PepsiCo (PEP), Lockheed Martin (LMT), Merck & Co (MRK), Chevron (CVX), and The Home Depot (HD). These companies are not bad (at all!), yet simply victims of an environment with sluggish cyclical growth, a big focus on AI disruption, and issues like a weak housing market, defense disruption risks, consumer weakness, and other issues that I expect have created tremendous value for new investors.

The ETF has a total expense ratio of just 0.06%, $70 billion in assets, and a quarterly dividend payout with a yield of 3.8%. This dividend has a five-year CAGR of 10.4%. Its three-year CAGR is 7.8%, which I expect to last going forward, as I think the economy is simply too weak to support double-digit growth, at least for the time being.

In other words, I think SCHD is a great ETF to buy most of the "mainstream" dividend stocks without having to pick winners yourself. Just set it and forget it.

Now, obviously, after having discussed two ETFs that both yield less than the 5.5% target, we need to step it up. That’s where the covered call strategy comes in. Essentially, fund managers sell covered calls to improve a portfolio’s income. The risk is that stocks can be called away if their stock prices rise beyond the strike price. Luckily, this only caps the upside. As these ETFs actually own the underlying assets, they do not take risks that go beyond capping their upside.

However, capping upside is also a risk, especially for investors who require a decent total return. That’s why I am so incredibly careful when it comes to these ETFs, as there are countless ETFs on the market that I expect to result in a total disaster for investors due to eroding portfolios, as too many stocks get called away. I have zero interest in higher income if it means hurting one’s principal. Zero.

That’s where the Amplify CWP Enhanced Dividend Income ETF (DIVO) comes in. It was launched in 2016 and owns 25 holdings with an expense ratio of 0.56%. It has a five-star rating due to its well-balanced approach.

It has a monthly dividend payout with a current annualized yield of 4.5%. Long-term, its goal is to generate 2-3% income from its dividends and 2-4% income from option premiums. This would result in a yield of 4% to 7%, depending on the success of its strategy. Going forward, if market returns are subdued, I think this number could be close to 7%.

I also like the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ), which is another five-star covered call ETF with a focus on tech stocks. It has a total expense ratio of 0.4%, a 30-day SEC yield of 9.5%, and a focus on the market’s biggest tech stocks. While it may be silly to cap one’s upside potential on these, I think it’s OK for at least three reasons:

  1. We own many of these stocks in our CGDV ETF, which I just discussed.
  2. If I am right and Big Tech stocks start to increasingly compete with each other in the field of AI, their valuations could become an issue, making a covered call strategy a great tool for additional value generation.
  3. These stocks have higher volatility than the dividend stocks covered by SCHD and others. This improves option premiums (broadly speaking).

Moreover, even if investors had "thrown away" all JEPQ dividends, the total price performance has been quite good, which shows the fund did not sacrifice safety for income, which they could have easily done if they wanted to.

For example, this is what the Global X Russell 2000 Covered Call ETF (RYLD) with 11% yield looks like without dividends:

Some may disagree with me, but I have zero interest in owning or discussing a covered call ETF with this kind of erosion.

This brings me to the next step, which is midstream. While we could easily include midstream in the single-stock selection of this 50/50 model portfolio, I think it deserves a place in the ETF selection as well.

For that, I like the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP), which owns Master Limited Partnership midstream companies and yields 8.3%. I also like the Global X MLP & Energy Infrastructure ETF (MLPX), which owns both MLPs and C-Corp midstream assets. However, because it yields less than 5.0%, I am using AMLP for this article.

AMLP's biggest holding, Western Midstream (WES), has a weighting of nearly 13.0%. I like that, as it’s my favorite MLP midstream stock. If I (as a non-American) could invest in MLPs, I would own WES for sure.

The ETF has grown its dividend by 9.6% per year over the past three years, supported by maturing infrastructure assets, rising free cash flow, and lasting growth tailwinds like the natural gas demand boom (thanks to AI and LNG exports), a general focus on energy exports, and strong demand for pipelines in areas like the Permian.

If I were retired, I would spend most of my days buying and thinking about midstream stocks.

Please note that AMLP does not issue a K-1 form. Although all of its holdings do, it provides investors with a 1099-DIV. That also explains why its expense ratio is 0.85%, as the asset manager has to deal with these issues. However, in light of its yield, it’s an expense ratio I can live with, although it hurts my frugal heart a bit.

Last but not least, I would buy preferred equities, as these have bond-like characteristics due to fixed dividends. Because of those aspects, they tend to have a high correlation to long-term government bonds. After all, if government bond yields rise, investors usually demand a higher premium on corporate preferred equities as well. In this space, I like two ETFs. One of them is the VanEck Preferred Securities ex Financials ETF (PFXF), which, as the name already gives away, does not hold financial securities. I prefer this, as I really dislike investments in banks. To me, these are mostly cyclical institutions without moats. Sure, there are exceptions like JPMorgan (JPM), but generally speaking, I avoid them.

This ETF has a 0.4% total expense ratio, a yield of 6.5% and a focus on electric utilities, residential and commercial REITs, telecommunication companies, software, aerospace, and, to a lesser extent, food, computers, automobiles, and others.

Since its exposure, and including dividends/interest income, it has consistently beaten the long-term government bond ETF (TLT), which I expect to last going forward, as I think the Fed’s more dovish stance will push much more capital into high-quality income that provides investors with a great risk/reward and premium over lower-yielding government bonds. In general, I think this benefits high-quality dividend stocks like the ones owned by SCHD.

Now, before we get to the asset allocation part, let me show you the correlations.

As expected, the broader equity ETFs CGDV and SCHD have a market correlation of 95% and 82%, respectively. For DIVO and JEPQ, these numbers are 88% and 95%, respectively. This makes sense, as both CGDV and JEPQ own the stocks that have accounted for most of the market’s gains, although with better diversification. SCHD and DIVO are more focused on value.

AMLP has the lowest correlation of just 57%. PFXF has a 70% correlation.

Now, in order to get to a 5.5% yield in a way that makes sense, I went with this setup:

  • A growth and income foundation of CGDV and SCHD with a 40% weighting.
  • Income boosters consisting of DIVO and JEPQ with 35% weighting.
  • Specialized income from midstream through AMLP with a 15% weighting.
  • Hybrid income from preferreds with 10% weighting.

Now, the best thing is that we can easily make adjustments.

  • Need more growth? Reduce PFXF and AMLP for CGDV.
  • Looking for a higher income? Sell some CGDV and SCHD for JEPQ.
  • Looking to reduce volatility? Reduce JEPQ for PFXF or DIVO.

I did not include REITs. This is mainly due to the poor performance of ETFs like the Vanguard REIT ETF (VNQ) and the fact that I prefer to invest in single ETFs. I will discuss this in a future article, when I give you my favorite picks for the 50% equity part of the model portfolio.

As the word count of this article has already gotten out of hand, I’ll now give you my closing words.

Takeaway

Building a portfolio is hard, as we all have unique lives and different goals.

However, we have one thing in common, which is that we all desire consistent and reliable cash flows to provide for our daily needs, whatever these may look like.

That’s why we discussed the ETF side of the 50/50 portfolio strategy, which comes with a 5.5% average yield and a great foundation providing safety, income, and growth.

Even better, it’s really easy to make adjustments when needed, as these building blocks are diversified and based on either well-diversified equity portfolios, bond-like preferred equity, or covered call strategies conducted by some of the best managers in the business.

Now, I’m going to think about the other 50%, as I’m looking for the "perfect" equities for retirement that come with income, growth, and diversification (low-correlation) benefits that also provide us with the opportunity to make adjustments.

In case you have suggestions, let me know in the comments!

data.title: Warner Bros. Discovery: High Risk Of Unacceptable Bid
data.description:
Only a month or so ago, Warner Bros. Discovery, Inc. (NASDAQ:WBD) traded at sub-$12, and now the stock is up over $21 based on a potential bidding war for their media assets. The major hiccup here is that the potential bidders are unlikely to meet the targeted goal of management. My investment thesis remains Bearish on the stock, especially after this big rally on an unlikely acceptable bid.

Stock Market Oddity

Warner Bros. has long struggled to shift into the video streaming wars. The company had the promising HBO service when streaming became hot, but a couple of merger distractions and poor execution left the business behind the leaders.

The media giant is now in the process of spinning off the cable networks, and the whole potential bid from Paramount Skydance (PSKY) and others only appears to be another major distraction at this point. CEO David Zaslav has long pointed to valuations above $30 as the true valuation of the stock.

Media reports suggest Paramount has apparently only bid $23.50 per share with a hard stop of $25 due to the cash requirements from Larry Ellison, father of CEO David Ellison. Charles Gasparino of Fox Business reports the Ellisons have already had 3 bids rejected.

The Paramount bid has always faced major headwinds due to the stretched funding requirements. Larry Ellison has a net worth topping $300 billion based on his massive position in Oracle (ORCL), but Paramount is only worth $18 billion, while Warner Bros. is worth $50 billion and would require upwards of $60 billion plus massive debt assumption to acquire.

The other potential bidders are Netflix (NFLX) and Comcast (CMCSA). The much larger media companies are expected to face more regulatory scrutiny, whether due to size or President Trump's concerns.

Besides, Netflix co-CEO Greg Peters has made several attempts to distance the streaming service from a major media buyout. While the executive was less dismal on the recent Q3'25 earnings call, Mr. Peters was clear that the company saw large media deals as constant failures back at the Bloomberg Screentime conference earlier this month, as follows:

We come from a deep heritage of being builders rather than buyers. One should have a reasonable amount of skepticism around big media mergers. They don’t have an amazing track record over time.

Back on the Q3'25 earnings call, the co-CEO outlined the major reasons media mergers don't work as follows:

So watching some of our competitors potentially to bigger via M&A does not change in and of itself, at least our view on the competitive landscape. And we don't think it changes the substance of the challenge that our competitors face. Specifically, the range of activities that we and our competitors have to get great at has never been assembled in a single company before.

Think about producing film and TV shows across multiple genres in multiple languages and dozens of countries around the world. I'm trying to figure out how to incorporate the latest technology, including AI and Gen AI. We're trying to figure out how we build better product experiences that can serve consumers better around the world. How about customer acquisition and retention? How do we optimize global payments? How do we optimize global partnerships? There is so much and we want to get better at all of those things. Our competitors are seeking to get better, all those things, of course, as well.

But you have to do that by the hard work of developing those capabilities in the trenches day to day. You don't get there simply by buying another company that is also still developing those same capabilities.

In essence, Netflix wants to focus on customer acquisition and how to incorporate AI into media to build the business, versus spending an incredible amount of time working with lawyers and regulators on closing a merger and integrating a complex business like Warner Bros. Regardless, though, Paramount appears to be heading towards making a hostile bid.

Tough Math

Paramount has the access to the cash for the deal, but the cash is outside of the business structure. A family member having money doesn't exactly help Paramount and shareholders extract any value from the Warner Bros. deal.

At $25 per share, Warner Bros. costs $62.5 billion, plus the company has a net debt load of $30.7 billion. The enterprise value reaches an incredible $93.2 billion, while again, Paramount has a merger stock valuation of only $18 billion.

The Ellisons would, in essence, pay a substantial amount of the deal price via cash for new shares, with a large portion via a loan. At the same time, Paramount would take on the $30 billion in net debt from WBD and still issue new shares for upwards of 20% of the deal.

The proposed deal value, based on Paramount Skydance trading at $16.50, was as follows, according to LightShed Partners:

  • $50 billion - 3 billion shares issued to Ellisons via $30 billion cash, $20 billion personal loan.
  • $30.7 billion - WBD debt assumed by Paramount.
  • $12 billion - 735 million shares issued to WBD shareholders.

The math gets incredibly more difficult for the Ellisons to spend an increasing amount of cash in order to bid $30 and beyond for the business. Each additional $5 bid is another $12.5 billion that has to be financed by the Ellisons or new debt by Paramount.

Warner Bros. is forecast to only produce around $8.5 billion in annual EBITDA, which leads to substantially lower actual profits. The deal supposedly offers $3 billion in synergies, but Netflix and research in our prior article highlight how these synergies don't lead to higher stock valuations.

WBD is already trading at close to 10x EV/EBITDA. The Ellisons are unlikely to pay a substantial additional amount personally in order to pay a richer valuation for the stock.

The risk remains upwards of $9 downside back to $12 on a bid failing to materialize, due in a big part to the practical financing issues with such a small business trying to swallow Warner Bros. The media reports suggest an upside potential of possibly $4 now, with a very low probability of the media company accepting a deal for only $25.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that the major risk is for WBD to not reach an acceptable deal, leading to the stock slumping. Investors should use the recent strength in the stock to unload a position.

data.title: Blackstone Q3: Distributable Earnings Up +48% (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I rate Blackstone Inc. (NYSE:BX) a buy for capital appreciation-focused investors who are looking for long-term buy and hold investments. After reaching a high of $190 in recent weeks, the stock dropped to $155 after the Q3 earnings call, representing an opportunity, in my view.

Over the past 10 years, Blackstone has outperformed the S&P500 (SP500)and the NASDAQ on a total return basis, growing to be the world’s leading alternative asset manager.

The recently posted Q3 results showed double-digit increases in fee-related and distributable earnings, which bodes well for its dividend policy. Assets under management (AUM) passed $1.2 trillion, confirming success in their investments and an ability to retain clients long term. The AUM is also an indication of future fee-related earnings, so the stock is on the right trajectory to grow for the rest of the year.

Finally, the company will benefit from recent legislation that allows some company retirement plans to include alternative assets, unlocking Blackstone’s funds to enter the $12 trillion market. President Trump signed an executive order in August allowing private equity and other alternatives to be included in 401(k) plans. Blackstone’s existing evergreen funds are ideally suited for the purpose.

The most important factors that influence my opinion of the stock are

  • Currently pays a quarterly dividend yielding 2.55% annually, going ex-dividend on 11/4/25.
  • BX has beaten the last 4 earnings estimates in a row.
  • Q3 earnings achieved double-digit increases in fee-related and distributable earnings.
  • Blackstone’s more than $1.2 trillion in assets under management include global investment strategies focused on real estate, private equity, credit, infrastructure, life sciences, growth equity, secondaries, and hedge funds.
  • Legislation allowing alternative assets in retirement accounts sets BX in a good position to grow.

Seeking Alpha Quant rates the stock a Hold at 3.27 (as of 10/23/25). Note the key grades in Growth (A-) and Profitability (A-).

World’s Largest Alternative Asset Manager

Based in New York with over $1.2 trillion in AUM, Blackstone Inc. is an alternative asset management firm serving high-net-worth individuals and institutions. The company specializes in private equity, real estate, credit and insurance, infrastructure, life sciences, growth equity, secondaries, and hedge funds. It offers both public and private funds, such as Blackstone Real Estate Income Trust (BREIT) in which the company is the largest owner of commercial real estate. The company also provides capital markets services.

Q3 Earnings - Beat

On October 23, the company declared Q3 2025 earnings results, with the following financial highlights vs. prior year quarter:

  • Distributable earnings of $1.9 billion grew 48%.
  • Fee-related earnings (FRE) of $1.5 billion grew 26%.
  • Revenues of $3.09 billion were down -15.7%
  • Expenses declined -7.7% to $1.75 billion.
  • Net Income of $1.2 billion declined -20.94%
  • Net Income Attributable to Blackstone Inc. was $624.9 million, down -20.0%
  • Repurchased 200k shares in the quarter.
  • Distributed $1.6 billion in dividends to shareholders.

Overall, Blackstone posted outstanding growth in earnings, which follows the previous three quarters in exceeding estimates. In particular, the distributable earnings growth is an important metric to watch as it covers the dividend.

Blackstone’s AUM grew +12% to $1.24 trillion, as seen in the chart below. This is critical in my view, as it shows an indication of future fee-related earnings and proof of performance. The company is growing its clients' assets, and the clients are sticking with them. Perpetual capital reached $500.6 billion (or half a trillion), an increase of 15% year over year.

Performance To Date

An investment of $10,000 in BX over the past 10 years would have grown to $75,820, outperforming the financial sector index as represented by Sector SPDR Fund (XLF), which would have made $32,720. In fact, BX surpassed the S&P500 (SPY) and the NASDAQ market (QQQ).

The extraordinary growth is due to a number of factors, including the ability of the company to attract investors, performance in high return sectors such as private equity, and trust in management, who are global leaders in their field. Blackstone serves large institutional investors who commit large pools of capital, such as pension funds, sovereign wealth funds, and ultra-high-net-worth investors. Blackstone has carved out a reputation for having smart people on the team who deliver results ahead of peers. This outperformance has earned the stock a premium with expectations of continuing to beat the estimates and grow at a higher rate than the rest of the industry.

Valuation

As an asset manager, BX pays the dividend based on distributable earnings made in the quarter, not trying to keep the dividend at a certain rate. Therefore, the distributions are variable, as seen in the chart below. Overall, the trend shows an increasing dividend. In my view, BX is not a stock for income investors, but rather for growth, or capital appreciation over time.

Overall, the dividend distribution is growing as the company expands. For example, in 2014 the stock paid $1.88 per share, and is now expected to pay $4.12 per share in 2025.

In comparison to peers, BX is the largest asset manager by market capitalization, as seen in the chart below. The dividend at 2.6% is average, but the dividend growth rate of 14.8% is the best amongst peers. BX’s 10-year total return of 626% is the second highest within the group. Return on equity of 29% is the highest amongst peers. Given its leadership, it's not surprising, in my view, that its price-to-book ratio of 15.2 is at a premium to peers.

BlackRock, Inc. (BLK) is second behind BX in market cap but has the ability to quickly pass BX based on growth of ETFs, in my view. BLK shares similar impressive metrics, such as an 8% 5-year dividend growth rate, 14% ROE, and 331% 10-year total return.

KKR & Co. Inc. (KKR) distributes a tiny dividend, focused more on capital appreciation, with the highest 5- and 10-year total return amongst peers.

Brookfield Corporation (BN), similar to KKR, barely has a dividend but delivers strong 5- and 10-year total returns. Its return on equity (ROE) is the lowest of the group at 1.8%.

Brookfield Asset Management Ltd. (BAM) distributes the highest dividend at 3.2% and a high ROE of 21%.

The Bank of New York Mellon Corporation (BK) delivers strong metrics, including a 2% dividend and 230% 5-year total return.

Over the past 10 years, BX grew 658% on a total return basis, edged out only by KKR with a 771% total return. Each one of these companies is far above peers. One of the takeaways, in my opinion, is that size and scale matter in these financial companies. Also, the profitability of alternative investments such as private equity generates more value than public securities.

Outlook

Blackstone is well situated to continue the growth of its AUM under the current bull market, which will be further fueled by the expected rate cut at the end of October. Layering on the expected sell-in of alternatives into retirement accounts, Blackstone is expected to continue its winning run into 2026.

Risks To Rating

Under the current macroeconomic situation, highly valued stocks are at risk for profit taking if the market sells off, such as during a correction. Many guest speakers within the financial media are talking about an AI “stock bubble,” adding to the “wall of worry” about what might trigger a sell off. For example, recently President Trump triggered a sharp sell-off with a social media post expressing his displeasure with China about the tariff negotiations.

Bottom Line

I rate Blackstone – BX a Buy given the recent drop in price after announcing better-than-expected 3Q results. After trading at a high of $190, the stock dropped to $155, presenting an opportunity, in my experience. BX currently pays a dividend yielding 2.55%, going ex-dividend on November 4th.

Over the past 10 years, BX has outperformed all key stock indexes and most peers, achieving a 626% total return. Growth of AUM to $1.2 trillion has made it the world's largest alternative asset manager.

Going forward, BX is driven by the bull market and has strong potential to expand into retirement accounts such as 401(k) programs. Recent legislation passed by executive order in August, allows some companies to offer alternative investments, such as Blackstone’s evergreen funds, in company retirement plans. This represents a large opportunity given the $12 trillion retirement market.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: BUZZ Investing: BUZZ Index Surges As Fed Cut And AI Leaders Drive Gains
data.description:
Investors cheered a dovish Fed pivot and resilient tech earnings as U.S. equities extended gains, with retail-fueled sentiment lifting select high-growth names.

U.S. equities posted further gains during the recent period between index selection dates (September 11, 2025 – October 9, 2025, the “Period”), extending their late-summer momentum and pushing major benchmarks to new highs. The S&P 500 rose and the Nasdaq Composite outperformed, supported by optimism surrounding the Federal Reserve’s long-anticipated 25-basis-point rate cut in mid-September. While the decision was widely expected, the accompanying statement struck a notably dovish tone, with policymakers signaling that additional easing may be appropriate should labor conditions continue to soften. Large-cap technology stocks led performance once again, buoyed by resilient earnings and continued enthusiasm around artificial intelligence. Broader markets benefited from the perception that policy is now pivoting toward supporting growth, helping sustain the rally despite lingering geopolitical and trade-related uncertainty. The BUZZ NextGen AI US Sentiment Leaders Index (the “BUZZ Index”) returned 17.4% during the Period versus 2.3% for the S&P 500. Year to date, the BUZZ Index has risen 57.9%, compared to the S&P’s 15.7%.

Monetary policy developments loomed large. On September 17, the Fed delivered a 25 basis point rate cut to bring its benchmark to 4.00-4.25%, citing rising downside risks to employment and affirming expectations for more cuts this year. The statement noted that downside risks to the labor market have increased. Treasury yields broadly fell on the news. Labor data remained soft: the August payrolls miss and rising unemployment had already stoked easing expectations; the weak report still appeared to validate the Fed’s shift. Inflation data showed mixed signals, with core measures remaining sticky even as headline prints flirted with moderation. Tariff risk re-entered the equation, notably with new duties announced on lumber and furniture, complicating the outlook for goods inflation. Overall, the Period was defined by equities pressing higher under the tailwind of monetary easing, while macro crosscurrents around inflation and trade reminded markets that the path ahead may not be entirely smooth.

The BUZZ Index returned 12.14% during the month of September compared to a return of 3.65% for the S&P 500 Index during the same period. Year-to-date, the BUZZ Index leads the S&P 500 with returns of 45.21% and 14.83%, respectively, as of the end of September.

AST SpaceMobile and Intel Lead BUZZ Performance on Landmark Partnership Announcements

Shares of AST SpaceMobile (ASTS) surged 126.2% during the Period, leading contributors to BUZZ Index performance. The move followed the October 8 announcement of a definitive commercial agreement with Verizon (VZ) to offer direct-to-cell service to Verizon customers beginning in 2026, leveraging Verizon’s 850 MHz spectrum alongside ASTS’s space-based network. The deal built on successful voice, video, and messaging demonstrations using unmodified smartphones and was widely viewed as a milestone toward commercial rollout, helping re-rate growth expectations for the space-to-cell category.

Intel (INTC) rose 53.6% during the Period, supported by a series of high-profile announcements that may have reshaped the company’s AI and PC road map. On September 18, NVIDIA (NVDA) disclosed a $5 billion investment in Intel stock alongside a multi-year collaboration to co-develop data-center and PC products, including x86 system-on-chips integrating RTX GPU chiplets and deeper NVLink connectivity. The partnership was interpreted as a notable vote of confidence in Intel’s technology stack and potential role in next-generation AI platforms. Momentum may have also reflected the U.S. government’s previously announced 9.9% equity stake in Intel in late August, which aimed to bolster domestic semiconductor capacity and added to the sense of balance-sheet support heading into the fall.

Rocket and DraftKings Pull Back; Execution Risks and New Competition Pressure Shares

Shares of Rocket Companies (RKT) declined during the Period, ranking among the leading detractors to BUZZ Index performance. The weakness followed the completion of its acquisition of Mr. Cooper Group (COOP) on October 1, a transformative deal that created the largest mortgage originator and servicer in the U.S. While strategically compelling, investors may have been cautious about near-term integration risks, potential margin dilution, and execution complexity. Index rebalancing around the close added further technical pressure, as S&P Dow Jones Indices announced the removal of Mr. Cooper from the S&P SmallCap 600 and the inclusion of Western Union in its place. The shift, combined with subdued housing-market sentiment and persistent interest-rate uncertainty, may have contributed to selling pressure even as the combined platform positions Rocket for long-term scale advantages.

DraftKings (DKNG) was another notable detractor, retreating amid growing concerns over new forms of competition in online wagering. Shares fell sharply in late September after reports highlighted surging trading volumes on federally regulated prediction-market platforms such as Kalshi and Polymarket, which allow users to trade event-based contracts outside traditional state-regulated sports-betting frameworks. The platforms’ nationwide accessibility and favorable tax treatment drew attention from investors who viewed them as potential disruptors to the sports-betting duopoly of DraftKings and FanDuel. The decline erased much of the stock’s summer gains, as traders reassessed near-term growth prospects despite DraftKings’ continued expansion in legalized markets and steady progress toward profitability.

Collectively, these stocks reflect strong price momentum and elevated investor engagement. The exception is Webull Corporation (BULL), the online trading platform that went public in April through its merger with SPAC SK Growth Opportunities Corp. After an early surge which saw its shares rally from $11 to an intra-day high of near $80, the stock quickly retraced to the $11 range and has since mostly traded sideways. Sentiment, however, had remained positive, buoyed by favorable comparisons to Robinhood Markets, Inc. (HOOD), which has gained more than fourfold since spring. Amid this month’s wave of new entrants to the Index, none has drawn more attention than Opendoor Technologies (OPEN). Much of the renewed enthusiasm centered on Toronto hedge fund manager Eric Jackson, whose unconventional campaign to spotlight Opendoor’s “value” potential, including daily appearances outside rapper Drake’s Toronto residence holding a sign for the “$OPEN Army”, went viral across social media. Following an initial pullback to $2, the stock extended its advance through August and September, reaching over $10 per share before drifting lower. Sentiment across online platforms remains exceptionally strong, underscoring sustained retail engagement and optimism surrounding the company’s recovery narrative. This month, OPEN holds the highest positive sentiment score in the eligible universe and enters the BUZZ Index at the maximum 3% weight.
